topic: Embedded - no_std with the alloc Crate

turns.content: I'm getting a linker error when I try to compile my proof-of-conceit. The message says undefined reference to 'core::panicking::panic_impl' and it seems like I forgot something about the global allocator in no_std environments.

That looks like you're missing a basic requirement for #![no_std] builds. In Rust, when you disable the standard library, the panic implementation isn't automatically available unless you explicitly provide one. You need to add extern crate alloc; to your crate root so that the core::panicking module gets linked properly without relying on std's runtime hooks.

I added extern crate alloc but it still fails. I thought maybe I needed to manually implement panic, or perhaps I misunderstood how the global allocator connects to Vec and String operations in this context?

You're right to suspect the connection between allocation and panics. In no_std land with a custom heap allocator like embedded-alloc, every data structure that owns memory—Vec, String, HashMap—must use your allocator's implementation of Drop and Panicking traits. The extern crate alloc; line is mandatory because it exports the core module which contains panic_impl. Without it, the compiler cannot find symbols for things like Vec::new or String::from_utf8, even if you've defined a heap_allocator.

Okay so I added the extern crate and now I can compile basic types but still have runtime issues when my program tries to allocate large buffers. It seems like embedded-alloc isn't being picked up correctly in the build script?

The issue is likely that you're not explicitly telling Cargo which allocator to use during compilation. Even with extern crate alloc;, if your Cargo.toml doesn't specify [build-dependencies] or uses cfg-if checks, the fallback might still be using std's allocator. You should declare embedded-alloc as a build-dependency in your Cargo.toml and ensure your main.rs explicitly imports it so that the heap_allocator trait is resolved before any Vec or String operations occur.

I updated Cargo.toml with [build-dependencies] but now I get a different error. It says 'no rule matches' for some function call. Is this because my custom allocator isn't implementing the required traits correctly?

Yes, that's exactly it. Your heap_allocator implementation must fully implement Drop and Panicking traits to replace std's behavior. If you're missing a method or using an incorrect trait name from embedded-alloc, the compiler won't know how to handle memory cleanup during drop. For example, if your allocator doesn't correctly implement core::panicking::Panic, then any panic attempt will fail at compile time because there's no target for the signal.

I checked my code and it looks correct. But I still can't get Vec to work with embedded-alloc without errors. Is there something specific about how the alloc crate interacts with the standard library types that I'm missing?

The key is understanding that extern crate alloc; only brings in core, not std's internal structs directly. When you use Vec or String, they rely on the allocator trait implemented by your heap_allocator. If your implementation doesn't correctly implement Drop and Panicking for the specific version of embedded-alloc you're using, those types won't compile. You might also need to ensure your build script generates the correct cfg flags so that no_std paths are taken instead of std ones.

Ah, I think I see it now. But wait, earlier you said extern crate alloc; is enough for panic_impl. Why do I still need this extra setup? Isn't the global allocator supposed to be automatic once I declare it?

You're catching a subtle distinction here. The extern crate alloc; line provides access to core::panicking but doesn't automatically configure your heap_allocator's interaction with Vec and String. Those types still need the custom Drop and Panicking traits wired up correctly in your allocator implementation. It's not just about linking the symbols; it's about ensuring every allocation path through your code respects the embedded-alloc contract.
